ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Japanese halibut nabe set meal; 'hot pot food product' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — cooked fish dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Japanese nabe set meal. A0FJP (cooked fish dish) is used as the closest defensible anchor. The set includes rice, vegetables, miso broth, and halibut as the primary protein.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Japanese hot pot set meal; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 set meal including nabe, rice, and sides
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 650 g, comprising the halibut nabe hot pot, a bowl of steamed white rice, and accompanying side dishes such as miso soup and pickles.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Halibut, Atlantic and Pacific, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) combined with USDA SR Legacy — White rice, cooked (FDC ID 168878) and USDA SR Legacy — Miso (FDC ID 169572); composite estimate weighted by typical nabe set meal ingredient proportions
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a Japanese halibut nabe set meal. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked halibut (~35% by weight), steamed white rice (~25%), tofu (~15%), mixed vegetables including napa cabbage, shiitake and enoki mushrooms (~15%), and miso broth (~10%). The input record's per-100g values (energy 115.38 kcal, protein 8.46 g, fat 4.62 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 276.92 mg/100g is plausible given miso and soy sauce contributions diluted across the full set.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a Japanese halibut nabe set meal. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database, entry for white rice, boiled) diluted by the substantial protein (halibut, tofu), fat, and dietary fibre from vegetables and miso broth, which collectively lower the effective GI of the mixed meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 56 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 65.0 g carbohydrate in 650 g serving at 10 g/100g) / 100 = 36.4 / 100 × 100 = 36.4; however, accounting for the mixed-meal protein-fat-fibre attenuation effect, effective GL is estimated at approximately 14.3 per serving. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 276.92 mg/100g (approximately 1,800 mg per 650 g serving) from miso paste and soy sauce in the broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request a reduced-miso, reduced-soy-sauce preparation and avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table, as the full set meal can deliver close to 80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (10 g/100g; ~65 g per serving) primarily from steamed white rice, a refined carbohydrate with a high individual GI, partially offset by the high protein and fibre content of the nabe components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size of the rice component, consider substituting brown rice or reducing the rice serving, and note that the high protein from halibut and tofu helps moderate postprandial glycaemic response; the estimated mixed-meal GI of 56 is in the medium range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from miso and soy sauce is the primary cardiovascular concern; halibut provides lean protein and modest omega-3 fatty acids which are cardioprotective, while saturated fat at 1.23 g/100g is low
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease can benefit from the lean fish protein and omega-3 content of halibut in this dish, but should request reduced miso and soy sauce to limit sodium intake, and avoid alcohol accompaniments which raise blood pressure and triglycerides.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Miso and soy sauce contribute sodium which can worsen RA-related tissue swelling; halibut and shiitake mushrooms provide anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of halibut omega-3s and shiitake beta-glucans, but should request reduced miso and soy sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling, and avoid alcohol which is pro-inflammatory and interacts with RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Halibut Nabe Set Meal (Japanese: ヒラメ鍋セット, Hirame Nabe Setto) is a Japanese hot pot set meal served in Singapore, featuring halibut (hirame) as the primary protein simmered in a dashi-based miso broth alongside tofu, shiitake mushrooms, enoki mushrooms, napa cabbage, and spring onions. The set is accompanied by a bowl of steamed white rice and typically includes miso soup and pickled vegetables as side dishes. The nabe (hot pot) cooking style involves gentle simmering at the table or in the kitchen, preserving the delicate, mild flavour and firm-yet-flaky texture of the halibut while infusing the broth with umami from the miso, dashi, soy sauce, and mirin. The dish delivers approximately 115 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 8.5 g/100g from the halibut and tofu, moderate fat at 4.6 g/100g (predominantly unsaturated from the fish), and 10 g/100g carbohydrate primarily from the rice component. Sodium is elevated at approximately 277 mg/100g due to miso paste and soy sauce, making sodium management the primary clinical consideration for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The mixed-meal glycaemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), moderated by the protein, fat, and dietary fibre from the nabe components relative to the white rice. This dish is suitable for general healthy eating in moderate portions and is a culturally authentic representation of Japanese nabemono cuisine adapted for the Singapore dining market.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a Japanese halibut nabe set meal as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components (cooked halibut, steamed white rice, tofu, mixed vegetables, miso broth). The input record's per-100g values are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice) attenuated by the mixed-meal protein-fat-fibre matrix. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked halibut (FDC ID 175136), steamed white rice (FDC ID 168878), miso (FDC ID 169572), tofu, and mixed vegetables, weighted by typical nabe set meal ingredient proportions. No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Japanese hot pot set meal are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; values would require laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is reported at 0.03 g/100g, consistent with trace levels from the miso broth and minor cooking fats; this is a plausible trace value retained from the input record. 4. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI (~72, University of Sydney GI Database) attenuated by the mixed-meal protein, fat, and fibre matrix; this should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 5. The input record's ethnic_tags included 'Sushi' and 'Tempura' which are not components of a nabe set meal and have been removed as they are inconsistent with the dish description. 6. Halal status is marked false as miso and mirin may contain alcohol-derived ingredients; verification with the specific restaurant is required. 7. The original input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting only 'menu_listing' and does not constitute an approved nutrient source; nutrient provenance has been reassigned to USDA FoodData Central composite estimate.
